items.title: Understanding Nephrolithiasis Prevalence and Risk Factors
items.content: Nephrolithiasis is a widespread condition with increasing prevalence due to lifestyle changes. It's crucial for clinicians to recognize the epidemiological trends, including higher lifetime risk in men compared to women, and variations by race.
keyPoints: Up to 20% of men and 10% of women will develop at least one kidney stone during their lifetime.
Prevalence is approximately 50% lower among black individuals than whites.
Peak annual incidence in white men is ~3.5 cases/1000 at age 40, declining to ~2 cases/1000 by age 70.
Annual incidence in white women peaks at ~2.5 cases/1000 in their thirties and decreases to ~1.5/1000 by age 50.

items.title: Economic Impact and Importance of Preventive Strategies in Nephrolithiasis
items.content: The economic burden of nephrolithiasis is significant, especially since it affects individuals often during their working years. Prevention strategies are vital to reduce recurrence rates and associated costs.
keyPoints: Nephrolithiasis incurs billions in annual expenditures, primarily for surgical treatment.
Prevention of stone recurrence is essential due to high recurrence rates post-initial episode.
Recurrence should be considered when radiographic evidence shows a second stone, even if asymptomatic.

items.title: Conditions Predisposing to Nephrolithiasis and Their Systemic Implications
items.content: Nephrolithiasis is associated with several systemic conditions that predispose individuals to stone formation. These include gastrointestinal malabsorption disorders like Crohn's disease and gastric bypass surgery, primary hyperparathyroidism, obesity, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and distal renal tubular acidosis. Additionally, patients with a history of nephrolithiasis are more likely to have hypertension, gout, cardiovascular disease, cholelithiasis, reduced bone mineral density, and chronic kidney disease.
keyPoints: Gastrointestinal malabsorption (e.g., Crohn's disease, gastric bypass surgery) predisposes to stone formation
Primary hyperparathyroidism is a significant risk factor
Obesity and type 2 diabetes mellitus are associated with increased risk
Distal renal tubular acidosis contributes to nephrolithiasis
Associated conditions include hypertension, gout, cardiovascular disease, cholelithiasis, reduced bone mineral density, and chronic kidney disease

items.title: Pathogenesis of Nephrolithiasis: Supersaturation and Inhibitors
items.content: The pathogenesis of nephrolithiasis involves the concept of urine supersaturation, where the concentration product exceeds the solubility product. Despite most individuals having supersaturated urine with respect to certain crystals, inhibitors like urine citrate prevent stone formation in many cases. Recent findings suggest that calcium phosphate deposits at the thin limb of the loop of Henle and extends to the papilla, providing a site for further crystal deposition.
keyPoints: Urine is viewed as a complex solution with supersaturation being key
Supersaturation occurs when concentration product exceeds solubility product
Inhibitors like urine citrate prevent stone formation despite supersaturation
Calcium phosphate deposits at the thin limb of the loop of Henle and extends to the papilla
Most calcium oxalate stones grow on Randall's plaque, a form of calcium phosphate

items.title: Dietary Calcium Reduces Stone Risk
items.content: Higher dietary calcium intake is associated with a lower risk of nephrolithiasis, potentially due to reduced intestinal absorption of oxalate and subsequent lower urine oxalate levels.
keyPoints: Higher dietary calcium intake lowers stone formation risk.
Reduction in risk may be due to decreased urinary oxalate from reduced intestinal oxalate absorption.
Low dietary calcium increases stone risk and may affect bone density.

items.title: Impact of Oxalate on Stone Formation
items.content: Urinary oxalate is a strong risk factor for calcium oxalate stones, with dietary oxalate being a weaker risk factor. Efforts to reduce high oxalate intake can be beneficial.
keyPoints: Dietary oxalate is a weak risk factor; urinary oxalate is a strong risk factor for stone formation.
Absorption of oxalate may be higher in stone formers.
Reducing high dietary oxalate intake is recommended.

items.title: Fluid Intake and Stone Risk
items.content: Increased fluid intake reduces the risk of nephrolithiasis by increasing urine volume, with a significant increase in stone formation risk when urine output is less than 1 L/day.
keyPoints: Urine volume below 1 L/day more than doubles stone formation risk.
Fluid intake is crucial for preventing stones.
Certain beverages like coffee and tea are associated with reduced stone risk.

items.title: Why Increasing Urine Volume Reduces Stone Recurrence
items.content: Lower urine volume results in higher concentrations of lithogenic factors, increasing the risk of stone formation. Higher fluid intake has been shown to increase urine volume and reduce stone recurrence.
keyPoints: Higher fluid intake increases urine volume.
Increased urine volume reduces stone recurrence.

items.title: How Urine Calcium Affects Stone Formation
items.content: Higher urine calcium excretion is associated with an increased risk of calcium oxalate and calcium phosphate stones. The relationship between urine calcium levels and stone risk is continuous, making arbitrary cutoffs for hypercalciuria ineffective.
keyPoints: No defined threshold for abnormal urine calcium excretion.
Continuous relationship between urine calcium and stone risk.

items.title: The Role of Urine Citrate in Preventing Stones
items.content: Urine citrate acts as a natural inhibitor of calcium-containing stones. Lower excretion increases the risk of stone formation, influenced by factors like metabolic acidosis and dietary habits.
keyPoints: Citrate inhibits calcium stone formation.
Lower urine citrate increases stone risk.

items.subtopic: Nephrolithiasis | CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS
items.title: Understanding the Silent Nature of Stone Formation and Symptom Onset
items.content: Kidney stones often grow silently over weeks to months or even years before becoming clinically detectable. The onset of symptoms, such as pain or hematuria, does not indicate when the stone formed but rather when it moved into the ureter.
keyPoints: Stone formation can be asymptomatic for years or decades.
Symptoms typically arise from stone movement into the ureter.
The onset of symptoms does not reflect the timing of stone formation.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · Nephrolithiasis | CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Nephrolithiasis | CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS
items.title: Differential Diagnosis for Acute Renal Colic and Painless Hematuria
items.content: Acute renal colic presents with sudden unilateral flank pain, often accompanied by nausea or vomiting. Differential diagnoses include conditions that mimic symptoms, such as acute cholecystitis, appendicitis, diverticulitis, bacterial cystitis, and pyelonephritis.
keyPoints: Renal colic is characterized by sudden onset of unilateral flank pain.
Pain may radiate to the testicle or labium depending on stone location.
Differential diagnoses include acute cholecystitis, appendicitis, diverticulitis, and bacterial cystitis.
UTI with ureteral obstruction is a medical emergency requiring immediate intervention.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · Nephrolithiasis | CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: When to Use Imaging in Nephrolithiasis Diagnosis
items.content: In nephrolithiasis, diagnosis can often be made based on history, physical examination, and urinalysis without waiting for radiographic confirmation. However, noncontrast helical CT is the preferred imaging modality due to its high sensitivity and ability to detect small stones (as small as 1 mm) and visualize uric acid stones.
keyPoints: Noncontrast helical CT is highly sensitive and can detect stones as small as 1 mm.
CT allows visualization of traditionally 'radiolucent' uric acid stones.
Diagnosis may not require radiographic confirmation if clinical findings are suggestive.

items.title: Management and Indications for Intervention in Nephrolithiasis
items.content: For patients with nephrolithiasis, parenteral NSAIDs like ketorolac are as effective as opioids for symptom relief with fewer side effects. Urologic intervention is generally postponed unless there's evidence of UTI, a stone size ≥6 mm, anatomic abnormalities, or intractable pain. The choice of intervention depends on the stone's size, location, composition, and urinary tract anatomy.
keyPoints: NSAIDs are as effective as opioids for symptom relief with fewer side effects.
Intervention is indicated if there's evidence of UTI, stones ≥6 mm, anatomic abnormalities, or intractable pain.
Extracorporeal shockwave lithotripsy (ESWL) is less invasive but used less frequently; endourologic approaches are more common.

items.title: Why a detailed history is crucial for stone prevention?
items.content: A thorough patient history helps identify predisposing factors and potential etiologic contributors to nephrolithiasis, enabling targeted interventions.
keyPoints: More than half of first-time stone formers will have a recurrence within 10 years.
History should include episodes frequency, previous imaging, UTIs, gastric bypass surgery, gout, hypertension, diabetes mellitus, and family history of stones.
Dietary habits, calcium intake, high-oxalate foods, fluid intake, and supplement use are critical for evaluation.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · EVALUATION FOR STONE PREVENTION | HISTORY | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY EVALUATION

items.subtopic: EVALUATION FOR STONE PREVENTION | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
items.title: What does the physical exam reveal in stone prevention?
items.content: Physical examination can uncover systemic conditions contributing to nephrolithiasis and guide further diagnostic testing.
keyPoints: Assess weight, blood pressure, costovertebral angle tenderness, lower-extremity edema.
Look for signs of primary hyperparathyroidism and gout.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · EVALUATION FOR STONE PREVENTION | HISTORY | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY EVALUATION

items.subtopic: EVALUATION FOR STONE PREVENTION | LABORATORY EVALUATION
items.title: How do laboratory evaluations guide stone prevention?
items.content: Laboratory tests provide essential data on metabolic abnormalities and risk factors for nephrolithiasis, informing treatment strategies.
keyPoints: Measure serum electrolytes, creatinine, calcium, phosphorus, uric acid; PTH if indicated by abnormal calcium levels.
Urinalysis can reveal red/white blood cells, crystals indicating stone type and risk of new formation.
24-h urine collections measure volume, calcium, oxalate, citrate, uric acid, sodium, potassium, phosphorus, magnesium, pH, creatinine; two samples recommended due to variability.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · EVALUATION FOR STONE PREVENTION | HISTORY | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY EVALUATION

items.subtopic: IMAGING | PREVENTION OF NEW STONE FORMATION
items.title: Why Use Low-Dose Renal-Limited Noncontrast CT for Baseline Stone Burden?
items.content: Low-dose renal-limited noncontrast CT is recommended to establish the baseline stone burden if not performed during an acute episode. This helps avoid misinterpreting a residual stone as a new one, which could lead to unnecessary changes in preventive medical regimens.
keyPoints: Helical CT without contrast is the gold standard for diagnosing nephrolithiasis.
Low-dose renal-limited noncontrast CT should be considered post-acute episode to establish baseline stone burden.
Preventive regimen changes may be unnecessary if residual stones are mistaken as new due to suboptimal imaging.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · IMAGING | PREVENTION OF NEW STONE FORMATION

items.subtopic: IMAGING | PREVENTION OF NEW STONE FORMATION
items.title: How Should Follow-Up Imaging Be Tailored for Nephrolithiasis?
items.content: Follow-up imaging should be individualized, with CT used only if it will alter clinical recommendations due to its higher radiation dose. Renal ultrasound is preferred when minimizing radiation exposure is a priority, despite being less sensitive.
keyPoints: CT provides the best information but has a higher radiation dose than other modalities.
Follow-up imaging should be tailored to individual patients and only performed if it will change clinical recommendations.
Renal ultrasound is used to minimize radiation exposure, acknowledging its limitations.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · IMAGING | PREVENTION OF NEW STONE FORMATION

items.title: Dietary Management for Calcium Oxalate Stones
items.content: For individuals with calcium oxalate stones, dietary modifications can significantly impact stone recurrence. A diet rich in calcium (1200 mg/d) and low in sodium and animal protein reduces stone formation compared to a low-calcium diet.
keyPoints: Dietary calcium restriction is harmful; 1200 mg of calcium daily is beneficial.
Low intake of sodium and animal protein can reduce stone recurrence by ~50%.
Thiazide diuretics, in doses higher than those for hypertension, lower urine calcium excretion.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · RECOMMENDATIONS FOR SPECIFIC STONE TYPES

items.title: Role of Citrate and Uric Acid in Calcium Oxalate Stones
items.content: Increased citrate intake can inhibit stone formation. Foods rich in alkali increase urine citrate, while potassium citrate supplements are used when dietary changes are insufficient.
keyPoints: Citrate inhibits calcium oxalate stones; increased consumption of fruits and vegetables raises urine citrate.
Potassium citrate or bicarbonate supplements can be added if diet alone is inadequate.
Allopurinol reduces stone recurrence in patients with high urine uric acid levels, despite no direct association between uric acid and calcium oxalate stones.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · RECOMMENDATIONS FOR SPECIFIC STONE TYPES

items.title: Dietary Patterns for Reducing Stone Recurrence
items.content: Adopting the Mediterranean or DASH diet can help manage stone recurrence by promoting a healthier dietary pattern.
keyPoints: The Mediterranean and DASH diets are associated with reduced risk of stone formation.
These diets emphasize fruits, vegetables, low sodium, and limited animal protein.
Observational studies show an inverse association between these diets and stone risk.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · RECOMMENDATIONS FOR SPECIFIC STONE TYPES

items.question: What is the approximate lifetime prevalence of nephrolithiasis in men according to National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey data for 2007–2016?
items.explanation: The passage states that up to 20% of men will develop at least one stone during their lifetime.

items.question: Which demographic group has a ~50% lower prevalence of nephrolithiasis compared to whites?
items.explanation: The passage mentions that the prevalence is ~50% lower among black individuals than among whites.

items.question: What factor is suggested to contribute to the increasing global prevalence of nephrolithiasis?
items.explanation: The passage suggests that Westernization of lifestyle habits, such as dietary changes and increasing body mass index, likely contribute to the increasing prevalence.

items.question: Which of the following conditions is NOT listed as predisposing to nephrolithiasis?
items.explanation: Hypothyroidism is not mentioned as a condition that predisposes to stone formation.

items.question: What is the most clinically important inhibitor of calcium-containing stones?
items.explanation: Urine citrate is identified as the most clinically important inhibitor of calcium-containing stones.

items.question: Where does the process of stone formation hypothesized to begin in nephrolithiasis?
options: Thick limb of the loop of Henle
Thin limb of the loop of Henle
items.explanation: The process is hypothesized to begin at the thin limb of the loop of Henle.

items.question: Which dietary factor is associated with a reduced risk of nephrolithiasis due to its effect on lowering urine oxalate levels?
items.explanation: Higher dietary calcium intake reduces intestinal absorption of dietary oxalate, leading to lower urine oxalate and a reduced risk of stone formation.

items.question: Which nutrient's supplementation is associated with an increased risk of calcium oxalate stone formation in men?
items.explanation: Vitamin C supplements are linked to raised levels of oxalate in urine, increasing the risk of calcium oxalate stone formation in men.

items.question: Which dietary factor is associated with an increased risk of nephrolithiasis due to its effect on urinary excretion?
items.explanation: Higher sodium intake increases calcium excretion independent of calcium intake, raising the risk of stone formation.

items.question: Which beverage is associated with a reduced risk of nephrolithiasis according to observational studies?
items.explanation: Observational studies have found that coffee consumption is associated with a reduced risk of stone formation.

items.question: Which urinary factor is most directly influenced by dietary intake?
items.explanation: Dietary intake significantly influences urine oxalate levels, with higher dietary oxalate being a major contributor.

items.question: What is the effect of metabolic acidosis on urine citrate excretion?
items.explanation: Metabolic acidosis leads to increased reabsorption of filtered citrate, thereby decreasing its excretion.

items.question: Which condition is associated with a urine pH consistently ≤5.5?
items.explanation: A urine pH of ≤5.5 is associated with the formation of uric acid stones.

items.question: What is a common risk factor for nephrolithiasis that can be modified?
items.explanation: Lower urine volume is a modifiable risk factor for nephrolithiasis, and increasing fluid intake can reduce stone recurrence.

items.subtopic: Nephrolithiasis | CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS
items.question: Which of the following symptoms is NOT typically associated with a stone lodged in the lower part of the ureter?
options: Radiation of pain to the ipsilateral testicle in men or labium in women
Sudden onset of unilateral flank pain
items.explanation: Pain radiating anteriorly is associated with stones lodged in the upper part of the ureter.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · Nephrolithiasis | CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Nephrolithiasis | CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS
items.question: Which condition can be mistaken for acute renal colic if a stone lodges at the right ureteropelvic junction?
items.explanation: Symptoms may mimic those of acute cholecystitis when a stone lodges at the right ureteropelvic junction.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · Nephrolithiasis | CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Nephrolithiasis | CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS
items.question: What is an important consideration in patients presenting with urinary urgency and frequency due to a stone lodged at the ureterovesical junction?
options: The patient may be misdiagnosed with bacterial cystitis
Immediate surgical removal of the stone is required
The urine culture will likely be positive for infection
This presentation typically resolves without intervention
items.explanation: Urinary urgency and frequency can lead to an incorrect diagnosis of bacterial cystitis, despite a negative urine culture.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · Nephrolithiasis | CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which imaging study is considered most sensitive and appropriate for confirming the diagnosis of nephrolithiasis?
items.explanation: Noncontrast helical CT is highly sensitive and allows visualization of uric acid stones, making it the preferred imaging study.

items.question: What is a key advantage of using abdominal ultrasound over other imaging modalities for nephrolithiasis?
options: It can detect stones as small as 1 mm.
It provides information on hydronephrosis without radiation exposure.
It visualizes all parts of the urinary tract including distal ureter.
It is more sensitive than CT.
items.explanation: Abdominal ultrasound avoids radiation and can provide information on hydronephrosis, although it is not as sensitive as CT.

items.question: When should urologic intervention be considered for a patient with nephrolithiasis?
options: In all cases of colic regardless of stone size.
Only if the patient requests surgical removal.
If there is evidence of UTI, low probability of spontaneous passage, or intractable pain.
items.explanation: Urologic intervention should be considered if there is evidence of UTI, a stone unlikely to pass spontaneously (e.g., ≥6 mm), or intractable pain.

items.subtopic: EVALUATION FOR STONE PREVENTION | HISTORY | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY EVALUATION
items.question: Which of the following is NOT typically included in a detailed history for evaluating stone prevention?
options: Number and frequency of episodes distinguishing stone passage from stone formation.
Previous imaging studies, interventions, evaluations, and treatments.
Inquiries about medical conditions such as UTIs, gastric bypass surgery, gout, hypertension, and diabetes mellitus.
items.explanation: A family history of stone disease is relevant, not a family history of cardiovascular disease.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · EVALUATION FOR STONE PREVENTION | HISTORY | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY EVALUATION

items.subtopic: EVALUATION FOR STONE PREVENTION | HISTORY | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY EVALUATION
items.question: What should be assessed during the physical examination for nephrolithiasis?
options: Weight, blood pressure, costovertebral angle tenderness, and lower-extremity edema.
Skin color, hair texture, and nail health.
Eye movement, pupil response, and reflexes.
Muscle strength, joint flexibility, and coordination.
items.explanation: These are the relevant assessments for nephrolithiasis as they can indicate systemic conditions related to stone formation.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · EVALUATION FOR STONE PREVENTION | HISTORY | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY EVALUATION

items.subtopic: EVALUATION FOR STONE PREVENTION | HISTORY | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY EVALUATION
items.question: Which laboratory test is NOT routinely recommended for initial evaluation of nephrolithiasis?
options: Creatinine, calcium, phosphorus, and uric acid levels.
items.explanation: Liver function tests are not part of the routine initial evaluation for nephrolithiasis.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · EVALUATION FOR STONE PREVENTION | HISTORY | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY EVALUATION

items.subtopic: EVALUATION FOR STONE PREVENTION | HISTORY | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY EVALUATION
items.question: What is a key reason to obtain at least two 24-hour urine collections before making therapeutic recommendations for nephrolithiasis?
options: To ensure the patient's fluid intake is adequate.
Because of substantial day-to-day variability in excretion of relevant factors.
To confirm the presence of urinary tract infection.
To measure the patient's daily caloric intake.
items.explanation: Day-to-day variability in urine composition necessitates multiple collections for accurate assessment.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · EVALUATION FOR STONE PREVENTION | HISTORY | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY EVALUATION

items.subtopic: IMAGING | PREVENTION OF NEW STONE FORMATION
items.question: What is considered the 'gold standard' diagnostic test for nephrolithiasis?
items.explanation: The gold standard diagnostic test for nephrolithiasis is helical CT without contrast.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · IMAGING | PREVENTION OF NEW STONE FORMATION

items.subtopic: IMAGING | PREVENTION OF NEW STONE FORMATION
items.question: Why might a preventive medical regimen be unnecessarily changed after an imaging study?
options: A residual stone is mistaken for a new stone
items.explanation: A preventive medical regimen might be unnecessarily changed if a suboptimal imaging study fails to detect a residual stone, which could then be mistaken for a new stone.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · IMAGING | PREVENTION OF NEW STONE FORMATION

items.subtopic: IMAGING | PREVENTION OF NEW STONE FORMATION
items.question: What is the primary reason renal ultrasound is typically used instead of CT in follow-up imaging?
items.explanation: Renal ultrasound is typically used to minimize radiation exposure, despite being less sensitive than CT.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · IMAGING | PREVENTION OF NEW STONE FORMATION

items.question: Which dietary recommendation is NOT beneficial for individuals with calcium oxalate stones?
options: A diet containing 1200 mg of calcium and low intake of sodium and animal protein.
Avoiding high-dose vitamin C supplements to reduce endogenous oxalate production.
Restricting dietary calcium intake to less than 400 mg/d.
items.explanation: Dietary calcium restriction is not beneficial and can be harmful for individuals with calcium oxalate stones.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · RECOMMENDATIONS FOR SPECIFIC STONE TYPES

items.question: Which medication class has observational data suggesting it may reduce urine calcium excretion but cannot currently be recommended solely for stone prevention?
items.explanation: Bisphosphonates may reduce urine calcium excretion but cannot be recommended solely for stone prevention due to lack of conclusive data.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · RECOMMENDATIONS FOR SPECIFIC STONE TYPES

items.question: What is a known strategy to reduce endogenous oxalate production in patients with common nephrolithiasis?
options: Restricting calcium intake to less than 400 mg/d.
items.explanation: Avoiding high-dose vitamin C supplements is the only known strategy to reduce endogenous oxalate production in common nephrolithiasis.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · RECOMMENDATIONS FOR SPECIFIC STONE TYPES

items.question: Which dietary pattern has observational data showing an inverse association with risk of stone formation?
items.explanation: The Mediterranean and DASH diets have observational data showing an inverse association with risk of stone formation.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · RECOMMENDATIONS FOR SPECIFIC STONE TYPES

items.statement: The incidence of nephrolithiasis is higher in white women than in white men at age 40.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that the peak annual incidence among white men is ~3.5 cases/1000 at age 40, while for white women it's lower (~2.5 cases/1000) in their thirties.

items.statement: Radiographic evidence of a second stone is considered a recurrence even if asymptomatic.
items.explanation: The passage states that most experts agree radiographic evidence of a second stone should be considered a recurrence, regardless of symptoms.

items.statement: Nephrolithiasis directly causes upper urinary tract infections.
items.explanation: While nephrolithiasis does not directly cause UTIs, an obstructing stone with a UTI is considered a urologic emergency.

items.statement: Recent studies suggest that calcium phosphate deposits at the thin limb of the loop of Henle.
items.explanation: It is hypothesized that calcium phosphate deposits at this site and contributes to stone formation.

items.statement: Low dietary calcium intake decreases the risk of nephrolithiasis.
items.explanation: Low calcium intake increases the risk of stone formation and may contribute to lower bone density in stone formers.

items.statement: Higher fluid intake is associated with an increased risk of nephrolithiasis.
items.explanation: Increased fluid intake increases urine volume, which reduces the risk of stone formation.

items.statement: Higher dietary calcium intake increases gastrointestinal oxalate absorption.
items.explanation: Higher dietary calcium intake actually reduces gastrointestinal oxalate absorption, thereby reducing urine oxalate.

items.statement: Urine pH does not influence the formation of calcium oxalate stones.
items.explanation: Calcium oxalate stone formation is not influenced by urine pH, unlike uric acid and calcium phosphate stones.

items.subtopic: Nephrolithiasis | CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS
items.statement: The onset of symptoms in nephrolithiasis provides insight into when the stone actually formed.
items.explanation: Symptoms do not provide insight into when the stone actually formed, as stones can grow silently for years.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · Nephrolithiasis | CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Nephrolithiasis | CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS
items.statement: A UTI in the setting of ureteral obstruction is not an emergency.
items.explanation: A UTI with ureteral obstruction is a medical emergency requiring immediate intervention.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · Nephrolithiasis | CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: The absence of hematuria excludes the diagnosis of nephrolithiasis.
items.explanation: Hematuria may be absent, especially if urine flow is completely obstructed by a stone.

items.statement: Excessive fluid administration has been shown to be beneficial in the management of nephrolithiasis.
items.explanation: Excessive fluid administration has not been demonstrated to be beneficial; maintaining euovolemia is recommended.

items.subtopic: EVALUATION FOR STONE PREVENTION | HISTORY | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY EVALUATION
items.statement: Stone composition analysis is unnecessary if a stone or fragment is available.
items.explanation: Stone composition analysis is essential and patients should be encouraged to retrieve passed stones.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · EVALUATION FOR STONE PREVENTION | HISTORY | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY EVALUATION

items.subtopic: EVALUATION FOR STONE PREVENTION | HISTORY | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY EVALUATION
items.statement: Specialized testing, such as calcium loading or restriction, influences clinical recommendations for nephrolithiasis.
items.explanation: Specialized testing like calcium loading or restriction is not recommended as it does not influence clinical recommendations.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · EVALUATION FOR STONE PREVENTION | HISTORY | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY EVALUATION

items.subtopic: IMAGING | PREVENTION OF NEW STONE FORMATION
items.statement: CT should be performed only if the results will lead to a change in clinical recommendations.
items.explanation: CT is recommended only when its results would alter clinical management due to higher radiation exposure.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · IMAGING | PREVENTION OF NEW STONE FORMATION

items.subtopic: IMAGING | PREVENTION OF NEW STONE FORMATION
items.statement: There are definitive thresholds for urinary constituents and calculated urine supersaturation in preventing stone formation.
items.explanation: The risk associated with urinary constituents is continuous, and there are no definitive thresholds; dichotomization can be misleading.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · IMAGING | PREVENTION OF NEW STONE FORMATION

items.statement: Excessive calcium intake (>1200 mg/d) is recommended to prevent calcium oxalate stones.
items.explanation: Excessive calcium intake should be avoided as it does not benefit and may harm individuals with calcium oxalate stones.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · RECOMMENDATIONS FOR SPECIFIC STONE TYPES

items.statement: Allopurinol has been shown to reduce stone recurrence in patients with high urine uric acid levels.
items.explanation: Allopurinol reduced stone recurrence in a randomized controlled trial for patients with calcium oxalate stones and high urine uric acid.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · RECOMMENDATIONS FOR SPECIFIC STONE TYPES

items.assertion: Prevention of nephrolithiasis recurrence is essential once an individual has had a stone.
items.reason: Published recurrence rates vary by definitions and diagnostic methods used.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why prevention of recurrence is essential.

items.assertion: The majority of calcium oxalate stones grow on Randall's plaque.
items.reason: Calcium phosphate deposits at the tip of the renal papilla, forming Randall's plaque.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Higher potassium intake is associated with a reduced risk of nephrolithiasis.
items.reason: Potassium-rich foods increase urinary citrate excretion due to their alkali content, which reduces stone risk.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why higher potassium intake reduces nephrolithiasis risk.

items.assertion: Supplemental calcium is associated with a lower risk of stone formation compared to dietary calcium.
items.reason: The discrepancy may be due to the timing of supplemental calcium intake or higher total calcium consumption leading to increased urinary calcium excretion.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; supplemental calcium may increase stone risk, unlike dietary calcium.

items.assertion: Higher urine volume decreases the risk of nephrolithiasis.
items.reason: Lower urine volume results in higher concentrations of lithogenic factors.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why higher urine volume reduces stone risk.

items.assertion: Primary renal calcium loss is a common cause of hypercalciuria.
items.reason: Hypercalciuria is often due to greater gastrointestinal absorption or bone turnover.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; primary renal calcium loss is rare. The reason is true as it explains more common causes of hypercalciuria.

items.subtopic: Nephrolithiasis | CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS
items.assertion: Renal colic pain typically subsides completely after the stone moves.
items.reason: The discomfort often begins with a sudden onset of unilateral flank pain that varies in intensity.
items.explanation: Renal colic is a misnomer because the pain does not subside completely; it varies in intensity.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · Nephrolithiasis | CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: EVALUATION FOR STONE PREVENTION | HISTORY | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY EVALUATION
items.assertion: A detailed dietary history is crucial in the evaluation of nephrolithiasis.
items.reason: Diet plays a significant role in stone disease, influencing factors like calcium and oxalate intake.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why a detailed dietary history is crucial.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · EVALUATION FOR STONE PREVENTION | HISTORY | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY EVALUATION

items.subtopic: EVALUATION FOR STONE PREVENTION | HISTORY | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY EVALUATION
items.assertion: A urine culture should be performed routinely in all patients with nephrolithiasis.
items.reason: Urinalysis can reveal red and white blood cells, indicating possible infection.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; a urine culture is only needed if there's concern about infection.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · EVALUATION FOR STONE PREVENTION | HISTORY | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY EVALUATION

items.subtopic: IMAGING | PREVENTION OF NEW STONE FORMATION
items.assertion: Consistently diluted urine reduces the likelihood of crystal formation.
items.reason: The urine volume should be at least 2 L/d to achieve this effect.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why consistently diluting urine helps prevent stone formation.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · IMAGING | PREVENTION OF NEW STONE FORMATION

items.assertion: Thiazide diuretics can reduce calcium oxalate stone recurrence.
items.reason: Thiazide diuretics lower urine calcium excretion, which reduces the risk of stone formation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · RECOMMENDATIONS FOR SPECIFIC STONE TYPES

items.assertion: High-dose vitamin C supplements should be avoided to reduce exogenous oxalate absorption.
items.reason: Vitamin C supplementation increases endogenous oxalate production, leading to higher urinary oxalate levels.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Nephrolithiasis · RECOMMENDATIONS FOR SPECIFIC STONE TYPES
